coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of Fe3O4/carbon dots for excellent water treatment applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ngoc, Nguyen Thi Bich
Hoang, Nguyen Minh
Hau, Le Thi
Tu, Le Tuan
Dang, Hai-Linh Thi
Vu, Hong-Ha Thi
Anh, Vu Ngo Ha
Phuc, Do Gia
Dao, Van-Duong
coredata.dc:description: The spatial structure of the solar absorber directly affects the evaporation efficiency in solar-driven water evaporator. Herein, Fe3O4/Cdots (Fe3O4/CDs) composite was first exploited to assemble 3D solar absorbers in solar-to-steam devices. The synthesized nanohybrid materials contain various types of hydrophilic functional groups including –OH, –COOH, –C–OH, and exhibit an extremely high light absorbance value of more than 85 % in the entire solar spectrum. Fe3O4/CDs solar absorber was self-assembled to form a sea urchin-like structure with a large specific surface area. Therefore, the evaporation rate and efficiency were 1.352 kg·m−2·h−1 and 95.3 %, respectively. In addition, the developed materials were also employed to construct a closed water treatment system, in which Fe3O4/CDs nanoparticles played roles in harvesting water and purifying Fe3+ ions and CDs can detect Fe3+ ions in water. The Fe3+ treatment efficiency of solar-to-steam system using 3D Fe3O4/CDs absorber reached the highest point at 95.3 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fe3O4/CDs
Solar steam generation
Water treatment

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The HR-TEM image of Fe3O4/CDs. (b) The EDS mapping element of the Fe3O4/CDs sample. (c) The XRD patterns of the Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/CDs samples. (d) The FT-IR spectra of the synthesized samples. (e) The UV–Vis spectra of Fe3O4/CDs and Fe3O4. (f) The hysteresis loops of the Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/CDs samples.
(a) A digital photo of the 3D Fe3O4/CDs absorber membrane; (b) A schematic diagram of the experimental setup for the solar steam generation system.

(a) The increase of surface temperature of different samples in the wet state (dash line) and dry state (smooth line); (b) The model describes the reflection of sun rays onto spikes; IR images of 2D and 3D structures under 1 sun illumination (c) in the dry state and (d) in the wet state.

The mass change of solar steam generation system in the dark (a) and under 1 sun irradiation (b); Photo digital images of the solar steam generation devices under 1 sun illumination after 0 s and 900 s (c); The evaporation rates of the recycled Fe3O4/CDs nanoparticles under 1 sun illumination after 10 cycles (d).

(a) The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CDs in the presence of different Fe3+ concentrations under the excitation of 340 nm. (b) The linear fitting curve of F0/F − 1 versus the concentration of Fe3+ ranges from 0 to 10 µM. (c) The photo digital images of different water samples under sunlight and UV light. (d) The PL spectra of different water samples under the excitation of 340 nm.
(a) Fe3O4/CDs nanoparticles are dispersed in water. (b) The collecting process of Fe3O4/CDs using a magnetic bar.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of Fe3O4/carbon dots for excellent water treatment applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The spatial structure of the solar absorber directly affects the evaporation efficiency in solar-driven water evaporator. Herein, Fe3O4/Cdots (Fe3O4/CDs) composite was first exploited to assemble 3D solar absorbers in solar-to-steam devices. The synthesized nanohybrid materials contain various types of hydrophilic functional groups including –OH, –COOH, –C–OH, and exhibit an extremely high light absorbance value of more than 85 % in the entire solar spectrum. Fe3O4/CDs solar absorber was self-assembled to form a sea urchin-like structure with a large specific surface area. Therefore, the evaporation rate and efficiency were 1.352 kg·m−2·h−1 and 95.3 %, respectively. In addition, the developed materials were also employed to construct a closed water treatment system, in which Fe3O4/CDs nanoparticles played roles in harvesting water and purifying Fe3+ ions and CDs can detect Fe3+ ions in water. The Fe3+ treatment efficiency of solar-to-steam system using 3D Fe3O4/CDs absorber reached the highest point at 95.3 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, humanity is facing an urgent freshwater crisis as a result of climate change and water pollution. Several innovations have been established to address the issue of humans’ fresh water needs. Current applications involve reverse osmosis, multi-stage flash distillation, vapor compression distillation, and so on [1]. These methods allow for the rapid production of vast amounts of fresh water. However, these electronic devices are expensive, complicated operations, and unsuitable for places with poor economic conditions [1–3]. Solar steam generation devices (SSGD) have become increasingly common in recent years, with huge potential for freshwater production due to their low cost, and ease of construction and use [4–10]. In addition, SSGD could be integrated to generate electricity [11,12], remove water pollutants [13], etc.

Various solar absorber structures have been developed to boost photothermal conversion efficiency from 1D, and 2D to 3D structures [8]. Generally, the fabrication process for 1D and 2D solar absorber structures is simple and does not waste too much time. However, most of the sunlight will be reflected on the flat surface of the 1D and 2D structures, leading to a waste of solar energy and a low evaporation rate [14]. In contrast, the 3D solar absorber structure demonstrates a high evaporation surface area, efficiently capturing sunlight while reducing reflected rays into the surroundings. This results in a significantly improved evaporation efficiency than conventional structures [14].

Several approach methods have been applied to fabricate 3D solar absorber structures. The first one is using the 3D printing method [15]. For example, Li et al. [16] exploited the 3D printing to fabricate multilayer 3D from carbonaceous materials such as carbon nanotubes, nanofibrillated cellulose, and graphene oxide. The 3D structure had a high porosity (97.3 %) and absorbed more than 97 % of wavelengths in the range from 200 to 1200 nm. The evaporation system showed an efficiency of up to 85.6 % under 1 sun irradiation. The second approach method is based on nature-inspired design materials [8,17]. For example, Jiang Gong et al. [18] successfully fabricated a solar evaporator device using a photothermal layer with a sandwich structure inspired by the antifreeze proteins in beetles. The top and bottom photothermal layers were made of MnO2-modified cotton cloth with an efficiency of 89.5 %. Meanwhile, the middle layer is composed of a phase change hydrogel/microcapsule hybrid which is used to store and release heat. Under 1 sun irradiation, the device shows a high evaporation rate of 2.67 kgm−2h−1 and can generate a stable output electricity power of 0.42 Wm−2. Sun et al. [19] carbonized a corncob to fabricate a 3D evaporated structure with high porosity. The system has an evaporation efficiency of up to 4.16 kg·m−2·h−1 under 1 sun irradiation. The last one is template-based fabrication such as aluminum foam [20], porous hydrogel [21], xerogel foam [22], etc. The 3D solar absorber structures are fabricated by 3D printing methods or utilizing the natural 3D structures of materials such as fruit and plants. After the synthesis process, the 3D solar absorbers are kept constant in shape during the application time.

These ready-to-use 3D structures are very convenient to apply in practical applications. However, due to the photothermal conversion materials being fixed to create 3D shapes, after many uses, the accumulation of salts and pollutants significantly reduces the evaporation efficiency of the system. The need to replace the photothermal conversion layer causes a huge waste of materials and increases operating costs.

Recently, Fe3O4 nanoparticles have been widely applied in photothermal membrane fabrication. Because of its low coercivity and high saturation magnetism [23], Fe3O4 nanoparticles can readily be organized to form a 3D spherical shape under the impact of an external magnetic field. This construction considerably increases sunlight absorption area, which improves evaporation efficiency [24]. Additionally, the magnetic qualities facilitate the filtering and recovery of materials for reuse. However, the light absorption capacity of Fe3O4 is quite low [25] and there are no hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of the materials which could hinder the water molecules being exposed on the surface of the photothermal conversion membrane, leading to a slow evaporation process. In addition, Fe3O4 nanoparticles easily undergo oxidization when exposed to air and brine water, which significantly downgrades the quality of the photothermal membrane [26]. In terms of water treatment, the removal capacity of Fe3O4 nanoparticles to heavy metal ions in polluted water is normally lower compared to other carbon-based photothermal materials.

To solve these problems, in this study, we successfully fabricated a 3D evaporation membrane with a sea urchin structure using nanohybrid Fe3O4/CDs particles in the presence of a magnetic field. In particular, magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles play an important role in shaping the 3D structure while CDs play a role in conducting water to the surface of the membrane and improving the ability to absorb sunlight. Furthermore, CDs increase the ability to remove heavy metal ions in water sources and prevent the oxidization of Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The 3D structure could be easily reshaped through the adjustment of magnetic field strength. Besides, the Fe3O4/CDs nanohybrid particles could be effortlessly detached from the evaporator to remove salts and contaminants attached to the surface before reusing. In addition, we also take advantage of CDs in heavy metal ion detection to build a closed system including polluted water detection, treatment, and harvesting of clean water.
Glucose, citric acid, FeCl3·6H2O, and FeCl2·4H2O (AR grade) were purchased from Xylong company.

Fe3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized from the co-precipitation method [27]. 0.2 mol FeCl3·6H2O and 0.1 mol FeCl2·4H2O were dissolved into 50 ml distilled water. Next, 50 ml NaOH 4 M solution was dropped into the above mixture. After that, the mixture was heated at 60 °C for 24 h. Finally, the mixture was centrifuged to collect Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The products were dried at 60 °C for 24 h and then stored in an ethanol solution for further use.

CDs were synthesized according to our previous research [28]. 2 g of glucose and 1 g of citric acid were dissolved into 15 ml distilled water. Then, the mixture was put into a 25 ml Teflon to prepare for the hydrothermal process.

The hydrothermal process took place for 12 h at 180 °C. After the process, the obtained solution was filtered through a Whatman paper 2 µm to remove large particles. The collected solution that contained CDs was labeled as CDs.

1 g of Fe3O4 nanoparticles was immersed into a 15 ml CD solution. The mixture was then stirred at 100 rpm at 80 °C for 2 days. After that, the Fe3O4/CDs nanoparticles were collected by a magnet and dried at 80 °C for 24 h.

The morphology of the nanomaterials was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEM 2100, JEOL) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4800). The surface functional groups were characterized by an Afnity-1 S spectrometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan) and UV–vis absorption spectra (UV 2450-PC, Shimadzu – Japan). The magnetic hysteresis loops of the materials were recorded on a vibrating sample magnetometer (Lakeshore 7404). The photoluminescence spectra of the CDs were recorded on a Fluorolog FL3 (Jobin Yvon Spex) spectrometer.

The morphology and microstructure of the hybrid nanomaterial were illustrated in the HR-TEM (Fig. 1 a) and SEM images (Fig. S1a). As can be seen, the Fe3O4/CDs nanohybrid has a homogeneous size distribution. The particle size was in the range of 17 to 22 nm (Fig. S1b) with an average diameter of 19.91 ± 0.14 nm.

To verify the presence of CDs on the surface of Fe3O4 nanoparticles, the EDS, XRD, and FT-IR methods have been exploited. As can be seen in Fig. 1b, the uniform distribution of C and Fe atoms confirmed the attachment of CDs onto the surface of Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Besides, the broad hump from 20 to 30 degrees illustrates the amorphous structure of CDs which is in agreement with our previous studies [28–30]. Differing from the amorphous CDs, Fe3O4 nanoparticles have the typical spinel structure with the presence of XRD peaks corresponding with the (2 2 0), (3 1 1), (4 0 0), (5 1 1), and (4 4 0) planes. Fig. 1d illustrates the diversity of functional groups on the surface of Fe3O4/CDs. The broad peak at 3500 cm−1 corresponds to the –OH group. The sharp peaks at 1650 and 1500 cm−1 were ascribed to the C=O and C=C groups, respectively. The peak at 1100 cm−1 originated from the C–OH group. Meanwhile, the vibration of Fe-O was recorded at 550 cm−1 [31]. It is worth noting that the presence of various hydrophilic groups (–OH and C–OH) on the surface of Fe3O4/CDs nanoparticles leverages capillary forces and fosters fluid flow on the surface of the sea urchin structure [32].

The light absorbance of both Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/CDs nanoparticles was confirmed by UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 1e). As can be seen clearly that CDs significantly enhance the light absorbance of the Fe3O4 due to the excellent light absorption of CDs in the wavelength from ultraviolet to infrared light [33]. In the range of 200 to 800 nm, the light absorbance value of Fe3O4/CDs was always higher than 85 %, meanwhile, the value of Fe3O4 gradually decreased from 85 % to under 75 %. The results show that the nanohybrid Fe3O4/CDs particles are suitable to apply in solar steam generation systems.

To verify the capability of assembling the 3D absorber structure under a magnetic field, the magnetic properties of the nanohybrid Fe3O4/CDs particles were characterized. As can be seen in Fig. 1f, the saturation magnetization (Ms) and coercivity (Hc) values of Fe3O4 were 40 emu/g and 60 Oe, respectively. Due to the presence of nonmagnetic CDs, the Ms value of Fe3O4/CDs material decreased to 37 emu/g, when the Hc value increased to 300 Oe. Overall, the Ms and Hc values of Fe3O4/CDs nanoparticles are excellent for the assembly of the 3D sea urchin structure.

Fig. 2 a shows the 3D Fe3O4/CDs absorber structures on styrofoam under a magnetic field. Fig. 2b illustrates the experimental setup for the solar steam generation system. We designed a water line using cellulose paper to direct water onto the surface of styrofoam. After that, Fe3O4/CDs nanoparticles were assembled directly onto the cellulose paper and styrofoam under the magnetic field created by a round magnet. Due to the low Hc value, Fe3O4/CDs nanoparticles could be easily assembled along the curve of the magnetic field to form the spikes. Under sunlight illumination, the mass of evaporated water was recorded by an electrical balance. The surface temperature of the membrane was recorded by a thermal camera.

Fig. 3 a shows the obvious difference in surface temperature of the 3D Fe3O4 absorber and 3D Fe3O4/CDs absorber under 1 sun irradiation. In the dry state, the 3D Fe3O4/CDs absorber reached the highest temperature at about 67 °C compared to only about 55 °C in the 3D Fe3O4 one. The difference was also shown in the wet state with the highest temperature of 3D Fe3O4/CDs and 3D Fe3O4-based light-absorption materials were about 39 °C and 32 °C, respectively. The results demonstrate that the presence of CDs significantly increases the surface temperature of the light-absorption materials which is also in agreement with the aforementioned UV–Vis result. The absorption spectrum of CDs is illustrated in Fig. S3. The absorption peak at about 270 nm originated from the π → π* transition due to the formation of sp2 conjugated networks. Due to the large number of π bonds in the C=C and C=O bonds inside the core and on the surface, CDs can aggregate to form π bond networks, which narrows the band gap of CDs. As a result, CDs can absorb almost the entire wavelength of sunlight [34]. When illuminated, the electrons in the π bonds are easily excited from π → π*, and the energy is then released as heat. Like CDs, nano Fe3O4 has a narrow band gap, and the electrons are also easily excited by wavelengths in the solar spectrum [35,36]. The excited electrons form electron-hole pairs. In returning to the ground state and recombining with holes, the electrons generate energy in the form of heat and lattice vibration. Furthermore, CDs can play as both an electron receiver and acceptor. So the interaction between CDs and Fe3O4 also plays an important role in boosting the nonradiative recombination of charge carriers which leads to the increase of the photothermal conversion of the Fe3O4/CDs membrane [37]. Furthermore, CDs also prevent the oxidation of the Fe3O4 from exposure to the oxygen in the air, as shown in Fig. S4.

Fig. 3a also illustrates the large difference in surface temperature between 3D and 2D structures. In the dry state, the temperature of 3D Fe3O4/CDs samples is approximately 15 °C higher than that value of 2D Fe3O4/CDs ones. Meanwhile, the temperature gap is maintained at about 3 °C in the wet state. The large temperature difference can be explained by Fig. 3b. In the 2D structure, the sun rays will be reflected out of the solar absorber after the first hit. Meanwhile, in the 3D structure, which was created under the magnetic field, the sun rays are reflected many times between the spikes. Therefore, most of the solar energy will be absorbed by the 3D structure, increasing the membrane’s temperature and speeding up the evaporation process.

The surface temperatures of 2D and 3D membranes were investigated using a thermal camera. As can be seen, the temperature of the 3D surface was much higher than the 2D structure in both dry (Fig. 3c) and wet states (Fig. 3d). The maximum temperature value of the 3D Fe3O4/CDs structure reached 67.5 °C in the dry state after 600 s illumination which was about 10 °C higher than the value of the 2D Fe3O4/CDs structure. In the wet state, the temperature difference was reduced by 5.4 °C. In addition, these Figures show the good light absorbance of Fe3O4/CDs compared to Fe3O4. In particular, the temperature of 3D Fe3O4/CDs is always higher than that of 3D Fe3O4 temperature in both dry and wet states.

The mass of water evaporation by the time of the solar steam generation systems is illustrated in Fig. 4 a and Fig. 4b. It is worth noting that the evaporation rate remarkably depends on the effect of the magnetic field. Under the condition of no magnetic field, the evaporation rates of the system were 0.182 kg·m−2·h−1 and 0.933 kg·m−2·h−1 in the dark and under 1 sun illumination, respectively. When the magnetic field was exerted, the evaporation rate increased significantly due to the formation of the 3D structure. The evaporation rates achieved 0.261 and 1.352 kg·m−2·h−1 in the dark and under 1 sun illumination, respectively. From these results, the solar efficiency of the 3D Fe3O4/CDs structure was calculated to be 95.3 % [38].

In a practical experiment (Fig. 4c), the 3D structure shows a promising result for real application devices. In the beginning, the cover layer was transparent. During the evaporation process, the water drops gradually formed onto the cover layer. After 900 s irradiation, the water drops grew and can be easily observed by the naked eye. The good reusability of the device was also demonstrated in Fig. 4d. After 10 times of disassembling and reassembling the 3D structure, there was only a slight change in the evaporation rate of the device.

The excellent heavy metal detection of CDs was used to measure the concentration of Fe3+ before and after the evaporation process. When Fe3+ ions appear, the lone pair of electrons on the functional groups –OH, C–OH, and –COOH on the CDs surface form complexes with Fe3+ ions through electrostatic forces [39–42]. Under UV irradiation, the excited electrons move non-radiatively to lower energy levels and fall into the energy levels of the unfilled 3d5 sub-shell of Fe3+ ions [39]. These electrons then release energy in the form of heat or vibration to return to the ground state, leading to fluorescence quenching on CDs. In the presence of Fe3+ ions, the PL intensity of CDs reduced significantly (Fig. 5 a). The quantitative measurement of Fe3+ concentration was determined according to the linear fitting curve between the F0/F − 1 and Fe3+ concentration (Fig. 5b).
y = 0.087 x - 0.007
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively. y is equal to F0/F – 1 and x is the concentration of Fe3+ ions.

After the evaporation process, the yellow color of the Fe3+ polluted water nearly disappeared after applying the 3D Fe3O4/CDs evaporator (Fig. 5c). Meanwhile, the yellow color can be seen in the condensate water if exploiting the 3D Fe3O4 one. Under UV light, the emission from the mixing sample of the condensate water sample filtered by the 3D Fe3O4/CDs absorber with CDs is as bright as the emission from the mixing sample of distilled water mixed with CDs, showing the low concentration of Fe3+ ions in the condensate water [4].

The PL spectra of the polluted water and condensate water samples were recorded in Fig. 5d. The value of F0/F – 1 was calculated to be 0.199 with F0 and F were 43.241 and 36.064, respectively. According to the linear fitting curve, the Fe3+ concentration of condensate water from 3D Fe3O4/CDs system was 2.372 µM with an adsorption efficiency of up to 95.3 %, which is much lower than that of harvested water from 3D Fe3O4 device is 8.246 µM with an efficiency of only 83.5 %. Thanks to the presence of various functional groups on CDs, chemical bonds between Fe3+ ion and CDs can be formed which greatly supports the adsorption capacity of the 3D Fe3O4/CDs materials.

The excellent magnetism of Fe3O4/CDs is illustrated in Fig. 6 . As can be seen, Fe3O4/CDs can be easily dispersed into water, thus becoming ideal for desalination when salt accumulates on the photothermal membrane's surface. After that, the materials were completely separated from the solution after 10 s by using a magnetic bar. On the other hand, Fe3O4/CDs can be easily recovered by washing and ultrasonic vibration multiple times after the adsorption process.

In summary, we have successfully fabricated the nanohybrid Fe3O4/CDs particles by incorporating Fe3O4 nanoparticles and CDs. The nanohybrid materials show good magnetic properties and light absorbance capacity. In solar steam generation application, the 3D structure was assembled by the Fe3O4/CDs nanoparticles with the exert of the magnetic field. The evaporation rate of the 3D structure device reached 0.261 and 1.352 kg·m−2·h−1 in the dark and under 1 sun illumination, respectively. In the case of steam revocation from seawater, the magnetic properties of Fe3O4/CDs nanoparticles are also suitable for salt desalination, allowing material to be revoked easily for reuse. Furthermore, the CDs also illustrate good results in quantitative and qualitative metal detection in water. By combining the applications of nanohybrid Fe3O4/CDs particles and CDs, a closed-water treatment was built, including pollutant detection, pollutant removal, and harvesting freshwater.

Nguyen Thi Bich Ngoc: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Nguyen Minh Hoang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Le Thi Hau: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Le Tuan Tu: Conceptualization, Supervision. Hai-Linh Thi Dang: Formal analysis. Hong-Ha Thi Vu: Methodology. Vu Ngo Ha Anh: Data curation. Do Gia Phuc: Data curation. Van-Duong Dao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Investigation, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Vietnam National Foundation for Science and Technology Development (NAFOSTED) (grant number 103.02- 2021.106).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2024.113254.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Emerging desalination technologies: current status, challenges and future trends
Desalination

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. E. Ahmed, A. Khalil, and N. Hilal, “Emerging desalination technologies: Current status, challenges and future trends,” Desalination, vol. 517, p. 115183, 2021/12/01/ 2021.

Anbar J. Eng. Sci.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Energy, environmental, and economic analysis of a new configuration multi-stage flash distillation unit coupled with steam power plant
Case Stud. Therm. Eng.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Mehtari, M. Kahani, and M. Zamen, “Energy, environmental, and economic analysis of a new configuration multi-stage flash distillation unit coupled with steam power plant,” Case Studies in Thermal Engineering, vol. 50, p. 103456, 2023/10/01/ 2023.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tilted 3D evaporator with high-performance salt rejection for seawater desalination
Desalination

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. T. Cuong et al., “Tilted 3D evaporator with high-performance salt rejection for seawater desalination,” Desalination, vol. 574, p. 117303, 2024/04/06/ 2024.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advancement in solar-driven interfacial steam generation for desalination: a state-of-the-art review
Energ. Technol.2301190

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. K. Hazra et al., “Recent Advancement in Solar-Driven Interfacial Steam Generation for Desalination: A State-of-the-Art Review,” Energy Technology, vol. 12, no. 3, p. 2301190, 2024/03/01 2024.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advances of 2D-enabled photothermal materials in hybrid solar-driven interfacial evaporation systems toward water-fuel-energy crisis
Adv. Funct. Mater.2304936

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. S. Irshad et al., “Advances of 2D-Enabled Photothermal Materials in Hybrid Solar-Driven Interfacial Evaporation Systems toward Water-Fuel-Energy Crisis,” Advanced Functional Materials, vol. 33, no. 51, p. 2304936, 2023/12/01 2023.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sustainable desalination device capable of producing freshwater and electricity
Desalination

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nature-inspired design for high-efficiency solar-driven water evaporation
J. Power Sources

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V.-D. Dao and H. T. K. Nguyen, “Nature-inspired design for high-efficiency solar-driven water evaporation,” Journal of Power Sources, vol. 609, p. 234676, 2024/07/30/ 2024.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances and challenges for water evaporation-induced electricity toward applications
Nano Energy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V.-D. Dao, N. H. Vu, H.-L. Thi Dang, and S. Yun, “Recent advances and challenges for water evaporation-induced electricity toward applications,” Nano Energy, vol. 85, p. 105979, 2021/07/01/ 2021.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances and challenges of emerging solar-driven steam and the contribution of photocatalytic effect
Chem. Eng. J.
